title: The 8-Hour Build, Traced
subtitle: One chief, seven builders, nine milestones, PR #36 merged. Every event reconstructed from the session's wire files — what actually happened, what held up, why the toggles still came out wrong, and the playbook that makes the next long run land clean.
subMeta: KimiCheckingWhatOtherChatDid · 4.7 MB of wire events · 63 screenshots · verified against merged main
footer: Verified live 20 Jul: toggles driven in the merged build on the isolated stack (:3042) — close/reopen both panels, 230→44px over ~700ms; claims cross-checked against merged main (ed26f1c9, 76 files, +10,533/−254). Read-only throughout — no Novakai-Command code was changed.

html: The run delivered: all nine of your fix-list items function in the merged build — I re-drove the app myself and the panels close, reopen, and glide at the house 700ms. The failure was narrower and more interesting: every check asked "does it work" — no check ever asked "does it match the design." Function got 63 screenshots and numeric probes; fidelity got zero side-by-sides. The toggle miss is exactly the kind of defect that survives that asymmetry. The 8-hour duration wasn't people standing around either — it was 626 model steps at ~20 seconds of latency each, run one builder at a time.

k: build window, green-light to final report
model steps · 739 tool calls · gates green at every commit
verification screenshots — 0 compared to the design
of builder wall-clock spent waiting on the model

label: Timeline · AEST
note: The one gold moment is the run's real inflection: you were awake, frustrated, and the process had no way to show you anything — only a chat fork that could explain. Gaps 19:29–22:07 and 03:31–07:58 are you away; the pipeline itself never idled.

label: 3 scenes shot · exact token inventory · 2 commits

html: First, the part that works: I drove the merged build myself. Both toggles close and reopen their panels, and the collapse interpolates 230→44px over ~700ms on the house curve — your round-2 complaint ("there is no toggle, the glyph opens a picker") is genuinely dead. What shipped wrong is fidelity, in two layers:

alt: Design reference: left rail header with clean two-pane toggle icon
cap: storyboard scene-01, left rail — one calm two-pane icon, hairline weight.

alt: Built app: left rail header with box-and-chevron toggle icon
cap: a box with a chevron-and-bar drawn inside. Right place, wrong glyph.

alt: Design reference: right panel header with matching two-pane icon
cap: right panel, the same icon mirrored. One icon family, both sides.

alt: Built app: right panel header with different chevron-box icon
cap: another invented variant. The pair no longer reads as a family.

alt: Built app with both panels closed: two orphan glyph buttons floating on bare background
cap: two orphan buttons floating on bare background — unframed chrome, which your own design law ("every zone in its own framed surface; flat-on-bg = FAIL") explicitly forbids. The storyboard never showed a collapsed state, so the builder invented one — and inventions default to generic-app idioms, not house taste.

label: Root Cause
headline: Design fidelity was asserted, never measured — the compare-to-reference step existed in the plan and died on its way to execution.
detail: You supplied hex-exact tokens and three reference scenes. They were used to build — and never to check. Sixty-three screenshots were taken; none was ever placed beside the reference. "Matches the storyboard's look" in the final report was recalled from a 5½-hour-old memory of the scenes, not a fresh comparison.

title: The plan
body: “Per-item verification (M9) compares against these refs, not vibes: M2 → rail chrome vs scene-01…” Explicit, per milestone.

title: Builder briefs
body: Refs listed as inputs — but every verification checklist asks functional questions only (“collapses at 700ms”), not “matches scene-01”.

title: The build
body: 3 of 7 builders ever opened a reference image — once, during recon. The toggle builder viewed scene-01 at 23:04, built until 23:51, never looked back.

title: M9 gate brief
body: The chief's brief dropped the refs entirely. The gate agent opened 0 reference images, read the token file 0 times. Checklist beat prose.

title: Final look
body: 3 static screenshots, no clicks, no refs re-opened. Verdict issued from memory. Your standing rule — click every control — wasn't applied at the finish line.

html: Second layer: even a perfect comparison couldn't have checked the collapsed state — the reference only shows three open-state scenes. No collapsed rail, no hover, no motion, no toggle-icon spec beyond 14 faint pixels. Wherever the spec ran out, builders invented; and nothing told them your taste laws, because those live in your head and chat history, not in any file a builder reads.

Not ceremony-theater, and nobody stood around — the wire timings say the run was latency-bound. Builders spent 93–98% of their wall-clock waiting on the model: median ~20s to first token, 626 steps across the run ≈ 4.2 hours of pure waiting. Every extra step — another screenshot read, another stack spin-up — bought ~20–25 seconds of latency on top of its actual work.

note: Share of each builder's wall-clock spent on model latency + streaming (from per-step wire timings). The tools — edits, tests, browsers — were nearly free.

html: So both readings of that night are the same fact. Your "9 gates for 1,500 lines" is directionally right — per-milestone ceremony (7× stack spin-ups, per-item screenshot rounds, per-commit gate runs) inflated the step count, and steps are what cost 20 seconds each. But the checks themselves earned their keep: the lint ratchet forced a real module restructure, tsc caught a dead import during your merge, the analyzer flagged a genuinely duplicated picker. Kill duplicated steps, not the checks. Sequential execution did the rest: seven builders in single file on a latency-bound workload is the slowest possible arrangement of the same work.

title: The plan carried the whole night
body: Verbatim owner fix list, root causes pre-established, per-milestone done-whens, boundaries and abort rules. Builders never re-diagnosed anything: the M3 builder went straight to reproducing your exact 1692×978 screenshot state, measured a 38,313px feed, and landed a 3-line fix with the mechanism written in the commit body.

title: Scope-fenced builders, fresh context each
body: Each brief: "You execute ONLY milestone X — other milestones belong to other builders." No file collisions, no scope drift, and M9's integration sweep found zero cross-milestone breakage. The chief never wrote code itself — it briefed, pushed after each landing, and kept ~116k context all night while builders stayed at 80–142k.

title: Root-cause culture, not workarounds
body: M6 traced Mission Control's empty DM list to the exact seam (rows gated on live-agent presence instead of feed history) and fixed it there in +39 lines. M4 added an honest "can't locate this row" note instead of a silent no-op. The squad-pill "bug" was proven data-blocked rather than papered over.

title: Environment hygiene held all night
body: After a previous round posted test messages into your live backend, every brief carried the isolated-ports recipe (3041/3042) plus three NEVERs. Seven builders, zero violations, and your :3031 app ran undisturbed through the entire build.

title: Honest reporting up the chain
body: The final report separated "dead" from footnotes: squad/pill empty because the test backend has no live agents; model.ts at 457 lines logged as debt; a cloned picker logged as debt. The merge conflict at 08:02 was resolved in main's favor and all gates re-run before the PR merged. Claims spot-checked against merged main today: all true.

title: Your two interventions measurably improved the inputs
body: Your precision challenge ("what exactly are you looking at vs me") forced crop-level evidence and caught a sloppy "header" ambiguity. Your exactness challenge ("we literally have the hex values") killed the editorial token distillation and produced the complete exact-value inventory — 23 colors, every weight and letter-spacing, per-class counts. Both commits happened within minutes.

title: No design-fidelity check existed anywhere in the run
body: Covered above. One process fact worth keeping: the requirement didn't vanish through defiance — it vanished through summarization. Each hop (plan → brief → checklist) compressed "compare against these refs, not vibes" a little further until the gate's checklist contained only functional items. Checklists beat prose; whatever isn't named in the checklist doesn't happen at 3am.

title: The design reference had no interaction states
body: Three static open-state scenes. No collapsed rail, no toggle glyph spec, no hover, no motion. Every gap became a builder invention (44px void strip, floating reopen buttons) — competent, generic, off-brand. Your taste laws — frame-everything, amber scarcity, calm density — are written nowhere in the repo a builder reads.

title: Sequential builders on a latency-bound workload
body: M4, M5, M6, M7 touch mostly disjoint files (context panel, rail pickers, missionControl, thread scroll). Run as three parallel worktree builders with the chief merging, the same work fits in roughly half the wall-clock. The ceremony duplication compounds it: each of seven builders re-spun the same backend+vite stack and re-read the same plan.

title: You were in the dark from 22:52 to 07:58
body:
Six milestones were pushed while you were awake and wondering. The side-channel fork could explain but not show — no screenshot, no milestone counter, no ETA ever reached you.

You · 02:34
"I just dont understnad what is happening for 8 hours like is everyone just standing around… this plan was a shit plan and shouldnt have 9 gates for 1500 lines of code"

A one-screenshot post per milestone push — the thing this app itself is being built to do — would have surfaced the toggle look by ~midnight and let you veto ceremony live, from bed.

title: The final inspection viewed; it didn't drive
body: The chief's closing pass took three static screenshots at your viewport and issued the verdict. Nobody clicked the toggles at the finish line, nobody re-opened the reference scenes beside the result, and motion claims rested on mid-run probes from hours earlier. Your own standing rule for UI — drive it exactly the way you would — was applied by builders mid-run but skipped at the moment that mattered most.

title: Small infra frictions, all now known
body: Background demo servers died at the 600s task timeout and again on stdin-close (vite exits when stdin closes — the fix is < /dev/null + no timeout, discovered live at 08:10). Briefs said @playwright/test but only playwright-core exists — every builder adapted, paying a few steps each. The Write tool couldn't reach /tmp, so scratch probes landed in the worktree and had to be cleaned before commits.

label: The Playbook For The Next Long Run

html: Your chief-delegation-method doc (written yesterday) already has the right spine — onboard, plan-then-stop, cross-provider audit, build with boundaries, verify lightly, close out. These eight bolt onto it; each traces to a specific failure above.

title: Spec states, not screens
body: Ten minutes before green-light: for every interactive control in scope, one line per state — open, closed, mid-motion, hover, empty. The storyboard can't show a collapsed rail? Then the plan says "collapsed = framed 44px strip, wordmark glyph, reopen chevron in the tab row." The toggle miss was unspecifiable-not-checkable; this makes it both.

title: Write the taste laws into the repo
body: A one-page docs/design-laws.md — frame-everything, amber scarcity, 700ms structural motion, calm density, show-don't-tell — linked from every UI brief. Builders invent wherever specs run out; make their default invention your taste instead of generic-app taste.

title: Make fidelity mechanical
body: Every UI milestone's evidence includes one composite image: built crop beside reference crop. The gate brief names each comparison explicitly ("M2 rail header vs scene-01 crop") — this run proved the checklist eclipses the prose above it. Assertion from memory doesn't count; a side-by-side file does.

title: Batch ceremony; keep the checks
body: One shared isolated stack for the whole run, owned by the chief. Browser sweeps at two or three seams instead of per-item rounds. Keep per-commit tsc/tests — they're 30 seconds and caught real bugs.
save: Target: cut ~200 of 626 steps ≈ 1.5h at observed latency.

title: Parallelize the disjoint middle
body: Chief maps milestones to files up front; disjoint ones run as simultaneous builders in separate worktrees, chief merges. This run's graph: M1/M2 → M3 → {M4, M5, M6, M7} → M8 → M9.

title: Checkpoint the owner in pixels
body: After every milestone push: one screenshot + one-line delta + "k of 9 · ETA" into #team. Costs the chief 60 seconds; gives you veto power from bed; kills the 02:31 black box. Show, don't tell — the app's own doctrine, applied to building the app.

title: The finish line drives, it doesn't view
body: Final inspection = click every control at the owner's viewport, capture motion as frame series, re-open the reference scenes fresh in the same context, and only then issue the verdict. If the finish-line agent can't click it, it isn't done.

title: Pin the infra in the brief
body: Demo servers: no timeout, stdin detached, from the first launch. Say playwright-core because that's what exists. Allowlist a scratch directory so probes never touch the worktree. All three cost steps this run; all three are one line in the next brief.

html: Kimi keeps a per-agent wire.jsonl — every prompt, thought, tool call, result, and per-step token/latency record. 4.7 MB of wire across 9 agents for this run (13 MB with screenshot blobs). I built a small tracer (wire-tracer.py, ~150 lines, lives beside this report) with three verbs, and they answer the three questions any long run raises:

cells: Collapses an agent's wire into a readable timeline — prompts, thinking, tool calls with compact args, result previews, todo snapshots, token counts per step. 8 hours reads in ~5 minutes per agent.
Where did time and tokens go?
Per-agent wall-clock, LLM-latency share, first-token medians, tool mix, token totals. This is what exposed the 95%-latency fact and the 20s step tax.

Cross-agent search over every event. Trace any requirement through plan → briefs → tool calls → reports and find the hop where it disappears. This is what convicted the M9 brief, and what showed which builders ever opened a design image.

html: That third move — chain-of-custody tracing — is the general audit technique for delegation pipelines: every hop that rewrites instructions is a place a requirement can silently die, so grep the requirement's key phrase at each layer's artifact and mark where it stops appearing. It took minutes to run and produced the headline finding of this review.
